content.subtitle: Core idea
items: Countable nouns have singular and plural forms. ✓ That **portrait** is striking. ✓ Those **portraits** are stunning.
Some countable nouns form irregular plurals, such as person/people and child/children.
A few nouns use the same form for singular and plural, such as sheep and deer.

content.subtitle: Pattern notes
items: With many hyphenated nouns, the main word is pluralised (e.g. mothers-in-law).
Collective nouns can take singular or plural verbs depending on whether the group is seen as one unit or as individuals.

content.subtitle: Group expressions
content.description: Certain countable nouns often appear in fixed phrases with of:
items: a flock of birds
a herd of cattle
a pack of wolves/cards
a bunch of flowers/grapes
a set of tools/keys

content.subtitle: Core idea
items: These nouns normally have no plural form and take singular verbs. ✓ The **equipment is** ready.

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: advice, baggage, bread, furniture, information, knowledge, music, progress, research, traffic

content.subtitle: Talking about a unit or amount
content.description: Use a partitive phrase + of when you want one specific item or quantity:
items: a piece of advice
a loaf of bread
a drop of water
a bit of luck
a sheet of paper
a grain of rice

content.subtitle: Core idea
items: Some nouns exist mainly in plural form and require plural verbs. ✓ Your **glasses are** on the desk.

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: binoculars, clothes, earnings, goods, jeans, outskirts, premises, scissors, shorts, surroundings, trousers

content.subtitle: Useful phrase
items: Use a pair of with two-part items (e.g. a pair of scissors, a pair of jeans).

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Some nouns ending in -s are still singular (e.g. news, economics).
A noun can be countable in one meaning and uncountable in another (e.g. paper = material / a paper = article).
Some normally uncountable nouns become countable in set expressions, such as a help or a knowledge of.

sections.title: Nouns with different meanings and uses

content.subtitle: Common shifts
items: A noun can be countable in one meaning and uncountable in another (e.g. paper = material / a paper = article).
Some normally uncountable nouns become countable in set expressions, such as a help or a knowledge of.

content.subtitle: Mainly with countable nouns
items: (a) few, several, many, both, each, either, neither

content.subtitle: Mainly with uncountable nouns
items: (a) little, much, a great deal of, an amount of

content.subtitle: Used with both types
items: some, any, enough, plenty of, a lot of/lots of, more, most, no, all

content.subtitle: Meaning note
items: a few / a little suggest a positive quantity; few / little suggest an insufficient one.

content.subtitle: Main uses
items: To mention one non-specific thing.
To introduce something new into a conversation.
To classify people or objects (e.g. She is a designer).

content.subtitle: Sound rule
items: Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds (e.g. a university, an hour).

content.subtitle: Main uses
items: For specific or previously identified people/things.
With unique references (the sun, the internet in some contexts).
With superlatives and ordinal numbers (the best, the second).
For groups understood in context (the rich, the elderly).

sections.title: Zero article: no article at all

content.subtitle: Main uses
items: With plural countable nouns when speaking generally (Books can change lives).
With uncountable nouns in general meaning (Patience is essential).
With many names of places, meals, subjects and languages (Madrid, breakfast, biology, French).

sections.title: Articles by category and with particular phrases and expressions

content.subtitle: Time
items: **a/an**: once a week, in an hour
**the**: in the morning, during the 1990s
**zero**: at night, on Monday, in July

content.subtitle: Places and geography
items: **a/an**: a river nearby
**the**: the Alps, the Nile, the Netherlands
**zero**: London, Mount Etna, Lake Geneva

content.subtitle: Institutions, transport and leisure places
items: **a/an**: take a taxi, catch a train
**the**: on the bus, at the station, go to the cinema
**zero**: go by train, be at school/work, go home

content.subtitle: Media, sport and study
items: **a/an**: write an essay, have a lesson
**the**: play the piano, listen to the radio
**zero**: play football, watch TV, study chemistry

sections.title: I: Original passage gap fill practice
sections.instructions: Complete the original passage using the best answers for the gaps.
sections.passage:
Original passage for Noun phrases and articles

Lena joined a library task because the group wanted fresh practice for passage gap fill practice. The tutor wrote (1) ...... (2) ...... (3) ...... (4) ...... (5) ...... (6) ...... (7) ...... (8) ...... (9) ...... on the board and asked everyone to explain their choices. Later, the group compared (10) ...... (11) ...... (12) ...... (13) ...... (14) ...... (15) ...... (16) ...... (17) ...... with the rule from the lesson. The text is new, but it practises the same passage gap fill.
